skill.name: vibe-security
skill.description: Security analysis skill for reviewing application code, dependency manifests, and common vulnerability patterns. It includes local analyzers, searchable vulnerability data, remediation templates, report generation, and an optional auto-fix workflow.
skill.summary: Review code and dependencies for common security vulnerabilities with local analysis helpers.
skill.author: 0x8506

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many severe patterns, but most are vulnerability signatures, remediation examples, or documentation snippets. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found. Real concerns remain because the skill runs local audit commands, can rewrite files, and generates HTML reports without escaping untrusted scan-result fields.

medium_findings.title: Unescaped HTML Report Content
medium_findings.description: The HTML reporter interpolates project names, vulnerability types, file paths, messages, code, and remediation text into HTML without escaping. If scan results include attacker-controlled markup, opening the generated report could execute script in the browser.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The report generator directly embeds fields from scan_results and vulnerability entries into HTML. I did not find escaping or sanitization before Path(output_path).write_text(html).

medium_findings.title: Auto-Fix Engine Can Rewrite Local Files
medium_findings.description: The auto-fix workflow accepts a file path and replacement text, creates a backup, then writes the replacement to the target line. This is intended functionality, but it can corrupt files if used on untrusted suggestions or incorrect paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly performs local file writes after a fuzzy line match. Backups reduce impact, but no project-root boundary or path allowlist is enforced.

medium_findings.title: Dependency Audit Runs External Package Manager Commands
medium_findings.description: The CVE scanner runs npm, pip-audit, Go, and cargo-audit commands against the selected project. Arguments are passed as lists, so command injection risk is low, but these commands may contact registries and expose dependency metadata.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Subprocess usage is confirmed and appears intentionally scoped to known audit tools. The main residual concern is network and privacy behavior from those tools, not shell injection.

low_findings.title: Static Findings in Vulnerability Pattern Data Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Many critical and high static hits come from CSV rows that define vulnerable examples, safe fixes, or regex signatures for detection. These entries include eval, pickle, API key, innerHTML, and command-execution strings, but they are data used by the scanner.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files are structured CSV knowledge bases with columns for patterns, examples, fixes, and explanations. The suspicious strings are not executed by these data files.

low_findings.title: Documentation Examples Trigger Dangerous Pattern Alerts
low_findings.description: The skill documentation intentionally shows vulnerable and secure code examples for education. Static findings for innerHTML, os.system, pickle, path traversal, and command execution in these sections are examples, not active skill behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown tables and fenced examples under security-pattern documentation. They are not executable instructions beyond demonstrating what to flag.

low_findings.title: MD5 Used for Backup Filename Only
low_findings.description: The auto-fix engine uses hashlib.md5 to shorten a file-content hash for backup names. This is weak cryptography if used for security, but here it is not used for integrity, authentication, or password storage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hash is truncated and included in a backup filename before shutil.copy2. I found no evidence that it protects a security boundary.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Evidence Found
low_findings.description: Targeted review did not find text asking evaluators to ignore instructions, override policy, skip security analysis, or claim pre-approval. The skill content stays within normal usage guidance and security documentation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: A targeted keyword review found no prompt-injection phrases. This is a negative finding, so confidence is high but not absolute.

dangerous_patterns.title: HTML Generation Without Escaping
dangerous_patterns.description: Untrusted scan-result fields are inserted into generated HTML report markup without an escaping helper.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The f-string inserts vulnerability fields directly into HTML. This pattern can create stored XSS in generated reports.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local File Modification Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The auto-fix engine copies a backup and writes replacement content to a chosen file path.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write path is direct and intentional. Risk depends on caller control and review process.

dangerous_patterns.title: Subprocess-Based Dependency Audits
dangerous_patterns.description: The dependency scanner invokes external package audit tools that may perform network requests.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are explicit and use fixed executable names. Network behavior comes from the audit tools rather than custom exfiltration code.

content.value_statement: Security reviews are slow when teams must inspect many languages and frameworks by hand. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through targeted scans, findings, fixes, and reports.
actual_capabilities: Runs Python AST checks for Python files and heuristic checks for JavaScript and TypeScript files.
Tracks tainted data from common input sources to dangerous sinks in Python and JavaScript.
Searches bundled vulnerability, rule, framework, and remediation CSV knowledge bases.
Runs dependency audit commands for npm, Python, Go, and Rust projects when matching manifests exist.
Generates HTML, JSON, CSV, and SARIF reports from existing scan-result data.
Can apply single-line fixes with local backup and rollback history.
limitations: The analyzers are lightweight and can miss framework-specific or multi-file vulnerabilities.
Dependency audit support depends on external tools such as npm, pip-audit, Go, and cargo-audit.
The auto-fix tool rewrites files and should be used with dry-run review first.
Some documentation examples reference scan commands that are not included in this skill package.

use_cases.title: Pre-release security review
use_cases.description: Check application code for injection, XSS, weak crypto, and authentication risks before a production release.

use_cases.title: Dependency risk triage
use_cases.description: Review package manifests and audit results to identify vulnerable dependencies and available fixes.

use_cases.title: Remediation planning
use_cases.description: Generate prioritized findings, safer patterns, and report artifacts for a security backlog.

prompt_templates.title: Quick vulnerability check
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner scan of one file or small folder.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use vibe-security to review this file for common security issues. Summarize critical and high findings first, then suggest fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Framework-focused review
prompt_templates.prompt: Use vibe-security to review my Express application for SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, authentication, and authorization issues.

prompt_templates.title: Dependency audit summary
prompt_templates.prompt: Use vibe-security to check this project dependencies. Explain vulnerable packages, severity, fix availability, and upgrade priority.

prompt_templates.title: Remediation and report workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review with fixes and evidence.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use vibe-security to audit this project, validate each finding, propose minimal fixes, and prepare a report for engineering review.

output_examples.input: Review a JavaScript API handler for SQL injection and XSS.
output: Critical: SQL query uses user input without parameters.
High: HTML output uses unsafe assignment with request data.
Recommended fixes: use parameterized queries and safe text rendering.
The npm audit found high severity dependency issues.
Upgrade paths are available for the affected packages.
Run tests after upgrades because transitive dependencies may change behavior.

output_examples.input: Create a security report for scan results.
output: Report includes severity counts, affected files, vulnerability categories, and remediation notes.
SARIF output can be imported into code scanning workflows.

best_practices: Run analyzers on a clean working tree so file changes and rollback history are easy to review.
Use dry-run mode before applying auto-fixes, then review diffs and run tests.
Treat generated findings as review inputs and confirm exploitability in the application context.
anti_patterns: Do not apply generated fixes directly to production branches without human review.
Do not treat pattern matches as proof of exploitability without checking data flow and trust boundaries.
Do not publish generated HTML reports with untrusted scan content unless report output is sanitized.

faq.question: Does this skill replace a professional penetration test?
faq.answer: No. It helps with static review and remediation planning, but it does not prove runtime exploitability.

faq.question: Which languages are covered best?
faq.answer: Python has AST analysis. JavaScript and TypeScript use heuristic checks and data-flow patterns.

faq.question: Can it change files automatically?
faq.answer: Yes. The auto-fix engine can replace a target line and create backups, so use dry-run first.

faq.question: Does it need network access?
faq.answer: Core code scanning is local. Dependency audit commands may contact package registries through external tools.

faq.question: Can it create compliance-ready reports?
faq.answer: It can generate report formats and map concepts to standards, but teams must validate compliance evidence.

faq.question: Why are some risky patterns bundled in CSV files?
faq.answer: They are examples and detection signatures used to identify vulnerable code, not active malicious behavior.
